summary: Basecamp is hiring a data analyst in a posting that doubles as a manifesto for the company's work philosophy. DHH emphasizes that while data informs decisions, Basecamp is not 'data-driven' — they drive with their heart. The role spans A/B testing, revenue forecasting, security forensics, and usage analysis, but the posting spends equal time selling Basecamp's culture: 40-hour weeks, remote work, generous benefits, and no office perks designed to keep people at work longer. Pay is benchmarked to San Francisco top-10% rates regardless of location, and the company explicitly prioritizes diversity and encourages 'imposters' to apply.
key_points: Basecamp uses data to inform decisions but rejects being 'data-driven' — data clears the head, but heart drives the company
The role is unusually broad: A/B testing, security forensics, sales tax analysis, cloud cost forecasting, and performance debugging
Remote-first with San Francisco-benchmarked pay regardless of location, rejecting the silicon valley office-perk model
The job posting is as much a cultural statement as a hiring ad, emphasizing calm work, 40-hour weeks, and sustainable pace
Basecamp explicitly seeks diversity and puts extra weight on candidates from underrepresented groups
money_quotes: Data is there to clear the head, but ultimately we drive the company with our heart.
You won't be looking for ways to squeeze the last sour drop out of the lemon at Basecamp.
This means the job isn't about maximizing revenue or minimizing costs. Yes, we want to make money and we don't want to be wasteful, but we also want to be kind, considerate, fair, flexible, and calm.

While we have a lovely office in Chicago, it's not where you'll find foosball tables constantly spinning, paid lunches, or any of the other trappings that companies use to lure employees into staying ever longer at work.
key_insight: A job posting can be a vehicle for company values — Basecamp uses hiring as an opportunity to articulate its rejection of data-obsession, overwork culture, and Silicon Valley office perks in favor of calm, heart-driven decision-making.
